text: The surgeon marks the position of the gracilis muscle by drawing a line from the ischial tuberosity to the medial condyle of the tibia, visually outlining its anatomical path.
The surgeon makes an initial skin incision eight centimeters below the ischial tuberosity, extending it through the subcutaneous tissue and deep fascia.

The surgeon mobilizes the gracilis muscle from the surrounding loose areolar tissue, separating it from adjacent muscles such as the adductor longus, using diathermy to cauterize small vessels encountered during the dissection.
The surgeon carefully navigates around the long saphenous vein and the medial cutaneous nerve of the thigh, ensuring these structures are preserved during the procedure.

The surgeon follows the distal tendon subcutaneously to its insertion point inferior to the medial condyle of the tibia. The overlying skin and subcutaneous tissue are incised, and the tendon is isolated from surrounding structures.
The surgeon confirms the identification of the correct tendon by pulling on the muscle belly, then divides the tendon using diathermy.
The muscle is reflected after being mobilized, revealing the underlying structures and allowing for further dissection.

The surgeon continues proximal dissection along the body of the muscle, with small perforating vessels visible entering the deep surface. These vessels are ligated to maintain the muscle's vascular supply.
The neurovascular bundle is visible entering the deep surface of the muscle, with the obturator nerve observed entering approximately two centimeters proximal to this location.

The surgeon repeats the tendon isolation process on the contralateral side, incising the overlying skin and subcutaneous tissue to expose and isolate the tendon inferior to the medial condyle of the tibia.
The surgeon highlights the vascular pedicle, making it visually prominent within the surgical field.

The surgeon uses diathermy to develop a tunnel between the peroneal defect and the medial compartment of the thigh through the deep fascia. Forceps are then passed through the tunnel to deliver the gracilis muscle into the peroneal defect.
The surgeon carefully positions the gracilis muscle to avoid twisting, ensuring the preservation of its blood supply during the transfer to the peroneal defect.
The surgeon closes the skin incision on the thigh in layers, with a visible subcutaneous suction drain of fourteen French gauge placed beneath the skin.

The surgeon weaves the gracilis muscles together and secures them to the distal sacrum and coccyx using two O monofilament permanent sutures. The central tendons of the unipennate gracilis muscles are positioned centrally, while the muscle bellies are placed laterally, facilitating a reconstruction with a central tendon. The muscles are then attached to the origin of the levator ani.
